title: Paalua leleole Austin & Rubinoff 2026
author: Austin, Kyhl A.
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-zero
description: Paalua leleole  Austin & Rubinoff, sp. nov.   http://zoobank.org/ urn:lsid:zoobank.org:act: 4308FD76-2528-4F37-979E-BA672FD96BEF  Figs 8G, H, J, L; 9D; 10D   Diagnosis.   Paalua leleole  is most similar to  P .  asteliana . Males of  P .  leleole  lack the strongly contrasting white or pale yellow scales along the veins of the FW found in  P .  asteliana .   Holotype,  ♂.  Maui:  T[he] N[ature] C[onservancy] Waikamoi Preserve, boardwalk platform; 20.7803, - 156.2274; 1845 m; 21 iii 2025; K. A. Austin, K. Faccenda, Z. Pezzillo; UVLED light sheet / DNA extraction KA1467 / KAA diss. #1411 (UHIM).  Paratypes  (18♂♂, 1♀).  Maui:  Haleakalā National Park, upper Kīpahulu Valley, Mauka Camp; 1950 m; 22 ii–5 iii 1984; B. H. Gagné; malaise trap / KAA diss. #0418 (BPBM). 4♂♂, same as previous except 2000 m; 10–16 vii 1983; F. G. Howarth, B. H. Gagné (BPBM). 2♂♂, Haleakalā National Park, upper Kīpahulu Valley, West Camp; 1920 m; 7–15 vii 1983; F. G. Howarth; malaise trap (BPBM). 1♂, Kīpahulu Val[ley]; 1950 m; 18 vii 1983 / ex lantern light / S. L. Montgomery (BPBM). 1♀, Koʻolau For[est] Res[erve], Waikamoi Flume Tr [ail]; 1300 m; 24 viii [19]77 / F. G. Howarth, G. K. Uchida /  Panaphelix  sp. nr.  asteliana  Sw [ezey]. Flightless! / KAA diss. #0419 (BPBM).   Description. Male (n=19).    Head.  Scales on ventral half of frons short, appressed, dark brown, nearly black; scales on dorsal half shaggy, long, dark brown; scales on vertex straw yellow, pale yellow anteriorly; labial palpus porrect, ca. 1.5× diameter of compound eye, dark brown, slightly paler at apex of third segment, iridescent purple when viewed at an oblique angle under magnification; medial surface pale brown or pale brown-yellow. Scaling on scape golden brown or yellow, with intermixed brown scales; flagellum strongly bipectinate; ramus ca. 4–6× width of flagellomere, unscaled; sensillae short, less than 0.1× length of ramus; dorsal scales of flagellum large, overlapping and not arranged neatly into rows, cream or pale yellow-brown; ventral surface of flagellum naked.  Thorax.  Scaling on dorsum with intermixed light brown and dark brown scales, arranged loosely into longitudinal rows; tegulae similarly colored, but scaling less orderly; collar light brown. Lateral surface of forelegs and midlegs dark brown, iridescent purple when viewed at an oblique angle under magnification, tarsal segments ringed with golden brown or yellow; hindlegs similarly colored, but paler. Dorsal surface of FW (FWL: 14.4–14.8 mm; FAR: 2.4; Fig. 8G) with ground color entirely mousy brown, except for slightly darker scaling at base of costa and small patch of black scales at end of cell; fringe pale brown. Ventral surface of FW entirely dark brown, iridescent purple when viewed at an oblique angle under magnification. Dorsal surface of HW pale brown, faint iridescent purple when viewed at an oblique angle under magnification, without strigulae; fringe concolorous. Ventral surface of HW similar to dorsal surface, slightly paler.  Abdomen.  Vestiture with dorsal scaling pale brown, slightly paler ventrally; dorsal pits present as described for genus. Genitalia (Fig. 9D) with uncus slender, finger-like, apically rounded, confluent with tegumen; socii slender, pendant, elongate, roughened, densely setose; tegumen robust, well-sclerotized, with tooth-like dorsomedial projection; arms of gnathos broad, laterally flattened, fused apically into large, evenly rounded terminal plate with dorsal flattened plate-like process; transtilla complete, slender, without spines or other modification; valvae broad, membranous, semicircular at apex, parallel-sided, angled at sacculus; sacculus weakly-developed, not distinctly more sclerotized than disc of valva; juxta plate-like, with deep V-shaped dorsomedial notch; phallus moderate, slightly downcurved, laterally expanded and slightly dorsoventrally flattened near apex; neither cornuti nor sockets observed.   Description. Female (n=1).     Head .  Scales on ventral half of frons mostly missing, only a few small scattered pale yellow or white scales remaining, scales on dorsal half loose, long, pale brown or cream; labial palpus porrect, ca. 2× diameter of compound eye, with long, loose scaling near apex of second segment, entirely pale brown, faintly iridescent purple when view obliquely under magnification, third segment yellow at apex; medial surface with scaling pale yellow or white. Scaling on scape cream; sensillae prominent, stiff, sharp, ca. width of flagellomere; dorsal scales of flagellum cream, densely overlaid, thinning apically.  Thorax.  Dorsum with intermixed brown and straw yellow scales, tegulae concolorous; legs similar to male, but slightly paler. Dorsal surface of FW (FWL: 12.6 mm; Fig. 8H); slender (FAR: 3.0), apically acute, with ground color pale brown, but much of the scaling heavily faded, the only brown scales apparent mostly at base of costa and between veins towards termen; fringe badly damaged, pale. Dorsal surface of HW white, gray at apex. Ventral surface of FW and HW similar to male, paler, with similar iridescent purple scaling when viewed at an oblique angle under magnification.  Abdomen.  Vestiture of abdomen similar to male, but dorsal scaling paler. Genitalia (Fig. 10D) with papillae anales large, elliptical, evenly roughened, setose; apophyses posteriores and anteriores both ca. 0.5× S7, straight; sterigma slender, well-sclerotized, bowl-shaped; ostium large, funnel-shaped; colliculum absent; ductus bursae short, straight, ca. 0.5× length of S7; corpus bursae weakly differentiated from ductus bursae, shriveled, ca. 1× length of S7 (likely much larger after mating); signum absent; ductus seminalis not observed.   Etymology.  From Hawaiian “lele,” meaning “flight,” and “ʻole”, meaning “without,” referring to the apparently flightless nature of females of this species.   Comments.  Frank Howarth collected “a long series from  Astelia  axils near Mauka Camp in Kīpahulu Valley at 1950–2000 m ” of this species. He noted that “all adults were resting in the axils and could not be coaxed to fly” (F. Howarth, pers. comm. 2025). We have not been able to locate these specimens, which are supposedly deposited in BPBM. The lone female known to us has oddly shaped wings, distinctly narrower, shorter, and more acute than other species of  Paalua , supporting the notion that females of  Paalua leleole  may be partly brachypterous and presumably flightless. Males appear to be fully flighted, as many of the paratypes were collected in a malaise trap and the holotype came to an elevated light sheet at night.   Biology.  The host plant of  Paalua leleole  is unknown, but we hypothesize that it feeds on the leaves of  Astelia menziesiana , in a similar fashion to  P .  asteliana . Adults have been collected resting on  Astelia  in Kīpahulu Valley (see Comments above).   Distribution.   Paalua leleole  is known from two widely separate localities on east Maui: Waikamoi Flume Road and Waikamoi Boardwalk on the northwest slope of Haleakalā between 1300–1850 m and Kīpahulu Valley on the southeast slope of Haleakalā at an elevation of 1950–2000 m.   Conservation status.  The conservation status of  Paalua leleole  is unknown. It appears to be restricted to intact wet forests on Haleakalā where  Astelia menziesiana  is a dominant component. Outside of the BPBM surveys of Kīpahulu Valley in the 1970s and 1980s, very little of this habitat has been surveyed for moths as it is largely inaccessible except by helicopter. The flightless nature of females of  P .  leleole  means that typical Lepidoptera survey methods (e.g., light traps) may not be effective in locating females of this species and instead visual searching and vegetation beating, especially in and around  Astelia menziesiana , may be required.
